interactions.summary: Jiang explains Frankism as liberation through taboo-breaking and then contrasts the mainstream view of Frankism as heresy with his alternative view that it may be a logical extension of chosenness.
The host offers his own interpretation of chosen people, and Jiang replies that repeated persecution likely produced cognitive dissonance that turned a mission of witness into a mission of rule.

claims.claim: Jiang says Jacob Frank taught that breaking taboo, including incest, proved radical faith by showing that divine trust was stronger than human convention.
Jiang says one interpretation of Frankism treats it as a natural extension of a superiority logic inside chosenness rather than merely as a heresy.
Jiang says centuries of persecution likely produced a form of cognitive dissonance in which Jews moved from wanting to be a light to the nations to wanting to rule the nations.

signature_moments.moment: Frank's feather-light act is lifting the burden of taboo off the mind so that people feel free to do anything.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It preserves Jiang's sharpest image for Frankist antinomian freedom.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang uses the Frankist idea that transgression itself can demonstrate faith and divine freedom.

uncertainty_notes.note: Jiang marks part of this packet as his own speculation about how persecution and chosenness interact, rather than as a settled historical account.
